cataloging_subject.label: The Early South-English Legendary: Laud Misc. 108 Selection
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloging subject is the edition-defined selection represented by University of Michigan item AHA2708.0001.001: sixty-three intellectual Middle English verse components edited by Carl Horstmann from the South English Legendary material in Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108, principally ff. 23r–198r, with the later-hand lives of Saints Blaise and Cecilia at ff. 228v–233v. It is not one homogeneous medieval work, the complete South English Legendary tradition, the whole Laud codex, a sole-witness diplomatic transcription, Horstmann's complete editorial carrier, the U-M encoding, or the repository XML.

catalog_summary.title: The Early South-English Legendary: Laud Misc. 108 Selection
catalog_summary.author: Multiple anonymous Middle English poets
catalog_summary.date: Layered medieval compilation, often placed c. 1270–1300; Laud main layer late thirteenth or early fourteenth century; appendix mid- or third-quarter fourteenth century; selection published 1887
catalog_summary.region: England; West Oxfordshire scribal dialect in the main Laud layer; proposed Worcester compilation and possible Oxford codex origin remain tentative

summaries.value: An edition-defined sixty-three-component selection from the Laud Misc. 108 recension of the South English Legendary. Its mostly calendrical sequence combines lives of saints, martyr legends, feast narratives, miracle and afterlife material, and a prologue in rhymed Middle English verse. The principal block is copied by hand A at ff. 23r–198r; Saints Blaise and Cecilia are a later hand-D appendix at ff. 228v–233v.

The XML has sixty DIV2 containers but represents sixty-three intellectual components: it splits Becket Life/Translation, the prologue/Fabian-Sebastian, both Julian lives, and the three Saint Michael parts at repertory boundaries, while merging the two XML divisions for Saints Philip and James. The count is therefore not a raw division count.

Horstmann uses Laud Misc. 108 as the principal running-text witness but explicitly supplies continuous lacunae from the Vernon Manuscript, Harley MS 2277, and Cotton MS Julius D IX, and reports a broad variant collation. The selection must not be described as a sole-witness diplomatic edition.

extent.description: Sixty-three intellectual verse components represented by sixty XML DIV2 containers: sixty-one components in the main Laud sequence and two in the later appendix. The repository XML has 146 LG and 16,877 nonempty BODY L elements. Its 544 PB elements map title and contents pages, omitted preliminary pages vii–l, and printed pp. 1–496; front-matter text on vii–l is not encoded.

content_parts.label: Laud Misc. 108 legendary selection
content_parts.summary: Container for the sixty-three intellectual verse components encoded from Horstmann's Laud-based running text: sixty-one in the main hand-A legendary and two later hand-D appendix lives.

locators.value: BODY/DIV1[1–2]; 60 DIV2 containers resolved into 63 intellectual components

content_parts.label: Main legendary sequence
content_parts.summary: Sixty-one intellectual components in the principal late-thirteenth- or early-fourteenth-century hand-A block, including separately indexed works that share XML divisions and the three-part Saint Michael sequence.

content_parts.label: Later-hand appendix: Saints Blaise and Cecilia
content_parts.summary: Two saint's lives from a distinct mid- or third-quarter-fourteenth-century hand-D layer, printed by Horstmann as an appendix after the main legendary.

content_parts.label: Encoded editorial frame and page scaffold
content_parts.summary: Horstmann's title page and contents plus page-break scaffolding for unencoded preliminary matter and the appendix heading; this paratext is not counted among the sixty-three medieval verse components.

locators.value: FRONT title page and contents; omitted preliminary PB vii–xlix plus bare PB l; BODY appendix heading
title and contents; preliminaries vii–l; appendix heading p. 484

notes: The XML carries 544 PB elements: title 2, contents 2, omitted preliminary vii–xlix 43, bare l 1, and printed pp. 1–496. It does not encode the text on preliminary pages vii–l.

evidence.citation: General Corpus pinned CME source XML, aha2708.xml.
evidence.summary: Exact evidence for identifier, edition title and source, U-M encoding statements, divisions, lines, notes, page milestones, headings, and the encoded-selection boundary.
Counted four DIV1, sixty DIV2, 146 LG, 16,877 nonempty BODY L, 544 PB, and zero BODY prose paragraphs; reconciled the sixty XML divisions to sixty-three repertory-defined intellectual components.

evidence.citation: Lineage evidence for Horstmann's complete 1887 edition and Internet Archive scan.
evidence.summary: Supports the edition title, publication, pagination, component boundaries, main Laud text, explicit supplied lacunae, collation, and editorial frame.

evidence.citation: Lineage evidence for Laud Misc. 108 catalog and complete Digital Bodleian facsimile.
evidence.summary: Supports the exact discontiguous target loci, hands, dates, physical context, scribal dialects, possible production place, rubrics, and visual boundary checks.

evidence.citation: Lineage evidence for Bell and Couch, eds., The Texts and Contexts of Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108 (2011).
evidence.summary: Supports the composite anthology model, layered South English Legendary chronology and geography debate, exact Laud contents and hands, and separation of work, scribal, and codex origins.

evidence.citation: Lineage evidence for DIMEV version 1.0 records representing the sixty-three components.
evidence.summary: Supports component identities, incipits, line extents, manuscript loci, subjects, and repertory boundaries, with noted discrepancies preserved.

evidence.citation: Lineage evidence for Tristan B. Taylor's 2023 study of Thomas Becket in the South English Legendaries.
evidence.summary: Supports the Becket component's genre and source context without turning that component study into evidence for every item.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item AHA2708.0001.001.
evidence.summary: Current official item page for stable identifier, publication information, electronic-text status, and public-domain access statement.

open_questions.question: Can individual poets, compilers, exemplars, and revision layers be distinguished more securely across all sixty-three components?
next_steps: Review component-level source studies and the full manuscript tradition without projecting a single-author model across the selection.

open_questions.question: What are the exact source-side subfolio endpoints for each continuous Vernon, Harley 2277, and Cotton Julius D IX passage supplied by Horstmann?
next_steps: Collate Horstmann's line transitions against full source images or authoritative transcriptions; preserve the established broad folio loci until then.

open_questions.question: Which manuscripts stand behind every abbreviated 'al.' reading in Horstmann's apparatus?
next_steps: Reconstruct Horstmann's complete sigla and apparatus corpus from the edition and manuscript comparisons.

open_questions.question: Why does DIMEV record 392 report twenty-six lines when the XML and printed incipit/explicit boundary yield twenty-two prologue lines?
next_steps: Check DIMEV's line-count method against the scan and record editor feedback if available.

open_questions.question: Does the Laud opening lack seven or eight legendary pieces?
next_steps: Reconcile the catalog contents footnote's mathematically implied seven missing numbers with the headnote's statement that eight pieces are lost.

open_questions.question: Why do the current Bodleian catalog and DIMEV description label item 17 Edward the Elder when its incipit and narrative identify Edward the Martyr?
next_steps: Consult catalog editors or item-specific scholarship; retain Edward the Martyr as the content label while recording the conflict.

notes: The selection boundary is editorial and composite: 'Part I' on the title page does not prove a missing narrative continuation, and the EETS notices project a broader standard collection, additions, glossary, and source study rather than a second half of this exact Laud text.
Manuscript date, scribal dialect, tentative codex production place, and reconstructed tradition-level compilation origin remain separate descriptive layers.
The source-cache files are research evidence and fixity records; their presence does not grant redistribution rights.
